---
title: "Project 1.1 — Least Squares · INSTRUCTOR SOLUTION"
subtitle: "One Problem, Three Lenses · Lens 1 of 3"
author: "Reference solution (instructor-only)"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-fold: false
    embed-resources: true
execute:
  warning: false
jupyter: python3
---

::: {.callout-important}
## Instructor-only reference solution
Do **not** distribute. From-scratch implementations live in `solutions/_reference.py`;
this notebook imports and applies them, fills every TODO, and records the real
NHANES results. See `solutions/VALIDATION.md` for the milestone-by-milestone check.
:::

```{python}
import sys, os
sys.path.insert(0, os.path.abspath(os.path.join(os.getcwd(), "..")))
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from info521 import data, plotting, checks
import _reference as ref

plotting.use_course_style()
ds = data.load_clinical()
age = data.primary_predictor(ds)
y = ds["y"]
print(f"{len(y)} patients · predictor = {ds['primary']} · target = {ds['target']}")

# Numerical conditioning: a raw age^9 column is ~1e17, so z-score age BEFORE the
# polynomial expansion. (A student who skips this hits a singular X^T X at high
# order — a teachable discovery, not a trick.)
agez = (age - age.mean()) / age.std()
```

## 1 · Design matrix

```{python}
design_matrix = ref.design_matrix          # columns [1, x, x^2, ..., x^p]
print(design_matrix(agez[:3], 3).round(3))
```

## 2 · Ordinary least squares — verify against the fixture first

```{python}
ols_fit = ref.ols_fit
fx = checks.LINEAR_FIXTURE
checks.expect_close(ols_fit(fx.X, fx.y), fx.w_ols, "OLS weights")
```

```{python}
# Fit age -> SBP (start with a cubic) and plot over the scatter.
order = 3
X = design_matrix(agez, order)
w = ols_fit(X, y)

grid_age = np.linspace(age.min(), age.max(), 200)
grid_z = (grid_age - age.mean()) / age.std()
yhat = design_matrix(grid_z, order) @ w

ax = plotting.scatter(age, y, label="patients")
plotting.fit_line(grid_age, yhat, ax=ax, label=f"OLS order {order}")
ax.set_xlabel("age (years)"); ax.set_ylabel("systolic BP (mmHg)"); ax.legend()
plt.show()
```

## 3 · Cross-validation from scratch

```{python}
orders = list(range(1, 10))
train_err, val_err = [], []
for p in orders:
    tr, va = ref.cv_polynomial(agez, y, p, k=5, lam=0.0, seed=521)
    train_err.append(tr); val_err.append(va)

ax = plotting.cv_curve(orders, train_err, val_err)
ax.set_xlabel("polynomial order"); ax.set_ylabel("RMSE (mmHg)")
plt.show()

best = int(np.argmin(val_err)) + 1
for p, tr, va in zip(orders, train_err, val_err):
    print(f"  order {p}: train {tr:.3f}  val {va:.3f}")
print(f"CV winner: order {best} (val RMSE {min(val_err):.3f}); "
      f"order-1 val {val_err[0]:.3f}, order-9 val {val_err[-1]:.3f}")
```

**Reading.** The validation curve is nearly **flat** across low orders and rises
again by order 9. On real NHANES the age→SBP signal is weak and close to linear
($R^2 \approx 0.14$, corr $\approx 0.38$), so the "underfitting at low order" arm of
the bias–variance story is **muted** — orders 1–3 are within ~0.01 RMSE of each
other on a ~16 mmHg scale. What *does* show cleanly is the **overfitting** arm:
order 9 has the lowest training error but the worst validation error. CV correctly
declines to reward the extra flexibility. (On the synthetic fallback, which was
tuned for order-3 dominance, the underfit arm is far more visible — see
`VALIDATION.md`.)

## 4 · L2 regularization

```{python}
ridge_fit = ref.ridge_fit
checks.expect_close(ridge_fit(fx.X, fx.y, fx.lam_ridge), fx.w_ridge, "ridge weights")

# Coefficient shrinkage at order 9 as lambda grows.
X9 = design_matrix(agez, 9)
for lam in [0.0, 1e-3, 1e-1, 1e1]:
    wl = ridge_fit(X9, y, lam) if lam > 0 else ols_fit(X9, y)
    print(f"  lambda={lam:<6}: ||w||={np.linalg.norm(wl):8.3f}  "
          f"max|coef|={np.max(np.abs(wl)):8.3f}")
```

As $\lambda$ grows the coefficient norm collapses — ridge shrinks the high-order
wiggle toward zero, trading a little bias for a lot less variance.

---

## Play artifact — the bias–variance sweep

```{python}
grid_orders = [1, 3, 9]
grid_lams = [1e-3, 1e-1, 1e1]
fig, axes = plt.subplots(3, 3, figsize=(11, 9), sharex=True, sharey=True)
for i, p in enumerate(grid_orders):
    Xp = design_matrix(agez, p)
    Xg = design_matrix(grid_z, p)
    for j, lam in enumerate(grid_lams):
        wl = ridge_fit(Xp, y, lam)
        _, va = ref.cv_polynomial(agez, y, p, k=5, lam=lam, seed=521)
        ax = axes[i, j]
        ax.scatter(age, y, s=6, alpha=0.15, color="0.5")
        ax.plot(grid_age, Xg @ wl, lw=2, color=plotting.OKABE_ITO["blue"])
        ax.set_title(f"order {p}, λ={lam:g}\nval RMSE {va:.2f}", fontsize=9)
for ax in axes[-1, :]:
    ax.set_xlabel("age")
for ax in axes[:, 0]:
    ax.set_ylabel("SBP")
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
```

**Interpretation (≈200 words).** Across the 3×3 grid the validation RMSE barely
moves (all ≈ 15.9 mmHg): age alone is a weak, near-linear predictor of systolic BP,
so there is little signal for extra capacity to capture. The bias–variance tradeoff
is still *visible*, just compressed. **Underfitting** is mild — the order-1 fit is a
gently rising line and already captures essentially all the recoverable signal.
**Overfitting** appears only at order 9 with weak regularization ($\lambda=10^{-3}$),
where the curve develops spurious wiggles at the data-sparse extremes of the age
range; its validation error is the worst of the grid. Increasing $\lambda$ visibly
**damps** that wiggle — by $\lambda=10$ the order-9 fit is nearly indistinguishable
from the order-1 line, because the penalty has shrunk the high-order coefficients to
near zero. The honest clinical reading: on this cohort, age explains ~14% of SBP
variance and a straight line is the defensible model; the value of the sweep is
showing students that CV *discovers* this rather than assuming it, and that
regularization buys insurance against the overfitting that flexibility invites. The
remaining 86% of variance is what the multivariate features of Project 2 (and the
other lenses of 1.2–1.3) exist to address.

::: {.callout-note}
## Gate
Gated by **Checkpoint 1 (normal equations)**.
:::

## Looking ahead

In 1.2 the same fit returns as the **maximum likelihood** estimate under a Gaussian
noise model — numerically identical weights.
